A-002-001-001: Silicon and germanium are the classic semiconductor elements: their conductivity sits between that of metals and insulators, showing both metallic and non-metallic behaviour. The distractors include galena (lead sulphide, an old crystal-detector mineral, not a base element here) and gold, which is a pure conductor, so neither pair fits the description of two semiconductor elements.
A-002-001-002: Gallium-arsenide (GaAs) lets electrons move faster than in silicon or germanium, so it can amplify and switch at very high speeds, which is exactly what microwave frequencies demand. That speed advantage is wasted at very low frequencies and GaAs is not chosen for high-power or ordinary bipolar-transistor roles, so microwave use is the answer.
A-002-001-003: Doping pure silicon or germanium with an element that has fewer outer electrons creates 'holes,' leaving fewer free electrons than the pure crystal; this is P-type material (P for positive holes). N-type is the opposite, having extra free electrons, so it is the tempting wrong choice.
A-002-001-004: Doping the crystal with an element that has extra outer electrons adds free electrons beyond what the pure crystal has, giving N-type material (N for negative carriers). P-type is the reverse, with fewer electrons and more holes, so it is the natural distractor.
A-002-001-005: In P-type material the dopant creates an excess of holes, so holes are the majority carriers and electrons are the minority. Free electrons are the majority carriers in N-type, not P-type, which is why that distractor is tempting; protons and neutrons stay locked in the nucleus and never carry current.
A-002-001-006: N-type material is doped to have extra free electrons, so free electrons are its majority carriers. Holes are the majority carriers in P-type material, making that the tempting wrong answer; protons and neutrons are bound in the nucleus and do not move as charge carriers.
A-002-001-007: In its pure (undoped) state silicon has almost no free charge carriers, so it conducts very poorly and behaves as a good insulator; the exam treats pure silicon as an insulator for this reason. Only after doping with impurities does it act as a useful semiconductor, so 'a semiconductor' is the tempting answer but here the key wants its pure-state behaviour, an insulator.
A-002-001-008: A semiconductor is named for exactly this behaviour: it conducts under some conditions and blocks current under others, sitting between an insulator and a conductor. N-type and P-type describe doped semiconductors specifically, and 'intrinsic' just means undoped, so 'semiconductor' is the general term being asked for.
A-002-001-009: Silicon is the standard material for making semiconductors because its four outer electrons form a crystal that can be doped to control conductivity. Copper is a conductor and sulphur is essentially an insulator, while tantalum is used in capacitors, so none of those make semiconductors.
A-002-001-010: In their pure state semiconductors like silicon have very few free charge carriers, so they conduct poorly and behave as good insulators. Only after doping with impurities do they become useful as semiconductor devices; tuned circuits and inductors are unrelated component types.
A-002-001-011: Doping means deliberately adding tiny amounts of impurity atoms to pure silicon or germanium to create extra electrons (N-type) or holes (P-type). Adding loose protons, ions, or electrons is not how doping works; it is the impurity atoms built into the crystal lattice that change its conductivity.
A-002-002-001: A Zener diode, operated in reverse breakdown, holds a nearly constant voltage across itself even as the current through it changes, which is why it is used as a voltage reference or regulator. The distractor about variable capacitance describes a varactor, not a Zener.
A-002-002-002: A varactor diode is designed so its reverse-biased junction acts like a capacitor whose value changes with the applied voltage, useful for voltage-tuned circuits and AFC. A Zener holds a constant voltage, so it is the tempting but wrong choice here.
A-002-002-003: The hot-carrier or Schottky diode switches very fast and has a low forward voltage, making it ideal as a VHF and UHF mixer and detector. A varactor would be used for variable capacitance and a Zener for a voltage reference, so those distractors describe other diode types.
A-002-002-004: Forward current heats the junction, and too much heat destroys the diode, so junction temperature sets the maximum forward current rating. Peak inverse voltage is a separate rating that limits reverse voltage, not forward current, so it is a tempting but incorrect choice.
A-002-002-005: Junction diodes are rated for the two stresses that can destroy them: maximum forward current (which heats the junction) and peak inverse voltage or PIV (the reverse voltage before breakdown). Reverse current and capacitance are not the defining ratings, so answers built around them are wrong.
A-002-002-006: Structurally, semiconductor diodes are either junction types (a P-N boundary inside the crystal) or point-contact types (a fine wire touching the semiconductor). Vacuum and electrolytic refer to tube and capacitor technology, not semiconductor diode construction.
A-002-002-007: A point-contact diode has very low capacitance because of its tiny contact area, so it works well as an RF detector at high frequencies. Its small junction cannot handle high voltage or steady current, ruling out the rectifier and constant-source distractors.
A-002-002-008: A PIN diode acts like a current-controlled resistor at RF, so with a DC bias it makes an excellent RF switch or attenuator. It is not used as a high-voltage rectifier or as constant current/voltage source, which are roles for other devices.
A-002-002-009: A Zener diode in reverse breakdown holds a steady voltage, so it is used to regulate (stabilize) voltage. It does not dissipate, decrease, or increase voltage or current as a primary purpose; maintaining a constant reference voltage is its job.
A-002-002-010: Power is P = V × I, so the current is I = P / V. With P = 50 W and V = 10 V, I = 50 / 10 = 5 amperes. The decimal-point distractors (0.5 and 0.05) come from misplacing the division, so always divide watts by volts to get amperes.
A-002-002-011: A Zener's power rating is given at 25 °C; raising the temperature leaves less safe margin for heat dissipation, so the power-handling capability becomes less. Components must be derated as they get hotter, so 'greater' or 'the same' is incorrect.
A-002-003-001: Alpha (α) is the ratio of collector current to emitter current, slightly less than 1 because a tiny part of the emitter current leaves through the base. The change relative to base current defines beta, not alpha, so that is the tempting wrong answer.
A-002-003-002: Beta (β) is the current gain measured as the change in collector current divided by the change in base current, typically tens to hundreds. The collector-to-emitter ratio defines alpha, so that distractor confuses the two key transistor parameters.
A-002-003-003: An NPN transistor conducts electrons from a negative emitter to a positive collector when its base is made positive relative to the emitter, the standard NPN bias. A PNP works with reversed polarities, so it is the tempting opposite answer.
A-002-003-004: Alpha is specified in common-base configuration, where it represents the forward current gain (collector current change versus emitter current change). It is a current relationship, not a voltage one, so the voltage-gain distractors are wrong.
A-002-003-005: The change in collector current with respect to base current is the definition of beta, the common-emitter current gain. Alpha is the collector-to-emitter ratio instead, so it is the natural distractor to watch for.
A-002-003-006: Alpha is defined for the common-base configuration, where the input is the emitter current and the output is the collector current. Common-emitter is where beta is defined, so it is the tempting wrong choice.
A-002-003-007: Beta, the current gain, is specified for the common-emitter or common-collector configurations, since both use base current as the input reference. Common-base is the configuration for alpha, so answers including it are incorrect.
A-002-003-008: A PNP transistor conducts when its base is made negative, with current flowing from a positive emitter to a negative collector, the mirror image of NPN operation. An NPN works with the opposite polarities (positive base, negative emitter), so it is the tempting wrong answer.
A-002-003-009: Alpha and beta are related by α = β / (1 + β); since beta is large, alpha works out just under 1. The other formulas misplace the plus or minus and the multiplication, so memorize this exact arrangement.
A-002-003-010: Common-emitter and common-collector configurations use base current as input and give high to very high current gain (beta, often 50 to several hundred). Common-base gives a current gain of only about 1 (alpha), so its gain is far lower, making the 'about double' or 'about half' answers wrong.
A-002-003-011: Beta is found from alpha by β = α / (1 - α); since alpha is just under 1, the small denominator makes beta large. The distractors swap the sign or use multiplication, so this is the formula to remember as the inverse of α = β/(1+β).
A-002-004-001: An enhancement-mode FET has no conducting channel at zero gate voltage, so no current flows until gate voltage is applied to create (enhance) a channel. This is the opposite of a depletion-mode FET, which conducts with zero gate voltage, so watch the wording carefully.
A-002-004-002: A depletion-mode FET is built with a channel already present, so current flows even with zero gate voltage; the gate voltage then depletes the channel to reduce current. An enhancement-mode FET is the opposite, having no channel until voltage is applied, so it is the tempting wrong answer.
A-002-004-003: A MOSFET's gate insulation is extremely thin and easily punctured by static charges or voltage spikes, so a built-in Zener diode clamps excessive gate voltage to protect that insulation. The diode is for protection, not biasing or substrate protection, so those distractors miss its purpose.
A-002-004-004: FET and CMOS devices have very thin gate insulation that static electricity can puncture, so they must be handled with anti-static precautions. They are not unusually light-sensitive or mechanically fragile, so static charge is the real hazard.
A-002-004-005: An FET controls current with a voltage on an insulated or reverse-biased gate, drawing almost no gate current, so its input impedance is high; a bipolar transistor needs base current, giving it low input impedance. This high-impedance input is a key reason FETs are chosen for sensitive front ends.
A-002-004-006: A JFET has three terminals: gate, drain, and source, analogous to the base, collector, and emitter of a bipolar transistor. Emitter/base/collector belong to bipolar transistors, so that pairing is the distractor to avoid.
A-002-004-007: JFETs come in two basic polarities: N-channel and P-channel, depending on whether the conducting channel carries electrons or holes. High/low power and silicon/germanium are not the defining categories of JFET type.
A-002-004-008: In an N-channel depletion MOSFET, conduction is by electrons moving through the already-present N channel, so it is simply n-channel depletion. The mismatched polarity and enhancement options describe other device types, so match the carrier (electrons → n-channel) and mode (depletion).
A-002-004-009: In an N-channel enhancement MOSFET, gate voltage creates an N channel and electrons then conduct, so it is n-channel enhancement. There is no real 'q-channel,' and the p-channel options use the wrong carrier polarity for electron conduction.
A-002-004-010: In a P-channel depletion MOSFET, conduction is by holes moving through the existing P channel, so it is p-channel depletion. Holes are positive carriers tied to P-type material, so the N-channel and nonexistent 'q-channel' options do not fit hole conduction.
A-002-004-011: In a P-channel enhancement MOSFET, gate voltage forms a P channel and holes then conduct, so it is p-channel enhancement. Hole conduction always goes with P-type material, ruling out the N-channel and 'q-channel' distractors.
A-002-005-001: An SCR has three terminals: anode, cathode, and gate, where a small gate pulse switches on the main anode-to-cathode current. Base/collector/emitter belong to transistors and gate/source/sink is not a real set, so anode/cathode/gate is the SCR's signature.
A-002-005-002: An SCR is a switch with two stable states: fully conducting (latched on) and non-conducting (off). It does not oscillate or split into NPN/PNP conduction modes as its stable states, so 'conducting and non-conducting' is correct.
A-002-005-003: Once triggered, an SCR conducts from anode to cathode just like an ordinary forward-biased junction (silicon rectifier) diode, with a low voltage drop. It does not behave like a Schottky, PIN, or varactor diode, which have different specialized characteristics.
A-002-005-004: When gated 'on,' an SCR latches into conduction and drops only a small forward voltage, behaving like a forward-biased silicon rectifier. When gated 'off' it blocks current, so the 'off' option is the opposite of what is asked.
A-002-005-005: An SCR is a four-layer PNPN device, which gives it its latching behaviour once triggered. The other layer orderings (NPPN, PNNP, PPNN) do not describe the standard SCR structure.
A-002-005-006: The control terminal of an SCR is the gate; a small current into the gate triggers the device into conduction. The anode and cathode carry the main current and 'emitter' belongs to transistors, so gate is the control element.
A-002-005-007: The SCR belongs to the thyristor family, the group of latching four-layer switching devices. Phase-locked loops, varactors, and varistors are unrelated device classes, so thyristor is the correct family.
A-002-005-008: In amateur gear the SCR's main use is in a power-supply overvoltage 'crowbar' circuit: if the voltage rises too high, the SCR fires and shorts the supply to blow a fuse and protect the equipment. Amplifier, preamp, and SWR-detector roles are not typical SCR applications.
A-002-005-009: The device with anode, cathode, and gate terminals is the SCR. Bipolar transistors use base/collector/emitter and FETs use gate/source/drain, so only the SCR has that exact terminal set.
A-002-005-010: Gated 'on,' the SCR latches and conducts with a small forward drop, just like a forward-biased silicon rectifier. The reverse-biased options describe the blocking, non-conducting state, which is the opposite of being gated on.
A-002-005-011: The SCR is the PNPN (four-layer) device among the choices, which gives it its latching action. The PIN, Schottky, and Zener diodes are all two-layer junction devices, so they are not PNPN.
A-002-006-001: A Class A amplifier is biased so the device conducts for the entire 360-degree cycle, never cutting off. This gives the best linearity but the lowest efficiency, which is the trade-off that defines Class A.
A-002-006-002: Class A conducts over the whole cycle, so it reproduces the waveform most faithfully, giving the highest linearity and least distortion. The trade-off is poor efficiency, but for low distortion Class A wins over AB, B, and C.
A-002-006-003: A Class AB amplifier conducts for more than 180 degrees but less than the full 360, a compromise between Class A (full cycle) and Class B (half cycle). This improves efficiency over Class A while keeping distortion lower than Class B.
A-002-006-004: A Class B amplifier conducts for exactly 180 degrees, half of each cycle, with the device cutting off for the other half. Two devices in push-pull each handle one half so the full waveform is reconstructed; less than 180 degrees would be Class C.
A-002-006-005: A Class C amplifier conducts for less than 180 degrees, only a portion of each cycle, which makes it very efficient but highly distorting. A tuned output circuit restores the sine wave, so Class C suits constant-amplitude (FM, CW) signals rather than linear amplification.
A-002-006-006: Class C conducts for the shortest part of the cycle (less than 180 degrees), so the device spends most of the time off and dissipates little power, giving the highest efficiency. Efficiency rises as conduction angle shrinks, so Class A (full cycle) is the least efficient.
A-002-006-007: CW, RTTY, and FM are constant-amplitude (non-linear) modes, so they tolerate the distortion of a Class C stage, which offers the highest efficiency. Linear modes like SSB would need Class AB, but for these modes Class C is the efficient choice.
A-002-006-008: Class A conducts over the entire cycle, so the device always dissipates power, giving the least efficiency of all classes. Efficiency improves as conduction angle decreases toward Class C, so Class A is the lowest.
A-002-006-009: Class C conducts for the smallest part of the cycle, so it reproduces only a sliver of the waveform, giving the poorest linearity and most distortion. It relies on a tuned circuit to rebuild the signal, so it is unsuitable for linear modes like SSB.
A-002-006-010: Class A is the amplifier that operates over the full 360-degree cycle without cutting off. Class AB conducts more than half, Class B exactly half, and Class C less than half, so only Class A covers the whole cycle.
A-002-006-011: Class C is the amplifier that conducts for less than 180 degrees of the cycle, the shortest conduction angle. Class B conducts exactly 180 degrees and Class AB more than that, so only Class C operates over less than half a cycle.
A-002-007-001: In a common-source FET amplifier the gate draws essentially no current, so the input impedance is set almost entirely by the external gate biasing resistor network. The source-drain and substrate resistances do not set the input impedance, so those distractors are wrong.
A-002-007-002: The output of a common-source FET stage is taken at the drain, so its output impedance is set chiefly by the drain (load) resistor. Supply voltages and the input impedance do not determine the output impedance, making those choices incorrect.
A-002-007-003: A Darlington pair (two transistors with combined gain) offers very high current gain, high input impedance, and low output impedance, ideal for driving loads from a weak source. The wording about 'mutual inductance' is nonsense for a DC-coupled transistor pair, so those distractors are wrong.
A-002-007-004: A common-base amplifier does not invert: its output is in phase with its input. The 180-degree (inverting) result belongs to the common-emitter stage, so that is the tempting wrong answer for the common-base configuration.
A-002-007-005: The common-base amplifier has a very low input impedance (the emitter input) compared with its high output impedance (the collector). This impedance step-up is a defining trait, so 'only slightly' higher or lower understates the large difference.
A-002-007-006: The common-emitter amplifier inverts the signal, so input and output are 180 degrees out of phase. Common-base and common-collector stages are non-inverting (in phase), so this 180-degree inversion is unique to the common-emitter.
A-002-007-007: The common-collector (emitter-follower) amplifier is non-inverting, so its output is in phase with its input, just following it with near-unity voltage gain. Only the common-emitter inverts by 180 degrees, so that distractor does not apply here.
A-002-007-008: The source-follower is just another name for the common-drain FET circuit, where the output is taken at the source and follows the gate voltage with near-unity gain. Common-source and common-gate are different configurations, so they are wrong here.
A-002-007-009: The common-source FET stage parallels the common-emitter bipolar stage: both invert the signal and provide good voltage gain. Common-collector and common-base behave differently, so common-emitter is the matching bipolar circuit.
A-002-007-010: The common-drain (source-follower) FET stage corresponds to the common-collector (emitter-follower) bipolar stage: both are non-inverting buffers with near-unity voltage gain and low output impedance. So common-collector is the correct match.
A-002-007-011: The common-gate FET stage corresponds to the common-base bipolar stage: both are non-inverting with low input impedance and are useful at high frequencies. So common-base is the matching configuration.
A-002-008-001: An operational amplifier is a high-gain, direct-coupled differential amplifier whose behaviour is set by the external components (the feedback network) you add around it. Its gain is not fixed by internal parts, and it is not just an audio amplifier or a software routine, so those distractors are wrong.
A-002-008-002: The ideal op-amp has infinite input impedance (draws no input current), zero output impedance (drives any load), infinite gain, and flat frequency response. Any option with zero input or infinite output impedance describes the opposite of ideal behaviour.
A-002-008-003: In a closed-loop op-amp the external feedback network sets the gain, because the op-amp's own huge open-loop gain is tamed by feedback to a precise, predictable value. The supply voltage and internal PNP details do not set the closed-loop gain, so those are distractors.
A-002-008-004: Offset voltage is the small input voltage difference that would be needed to make the output exactly zero, measured in the open-loop condition because of slight internal imbalance. It is not a closed-loop measurement or an output-minus-input figure, so those options miss the definition.
A-002-008-005: An ideal op-amp draws no input current, which means its input impedance is very high (ideally infinite). Fixed values like 100 or 1000 ohms describe real resistors, not the ideal op-amp's near-infinite input impedance.
A-002-008-006: An ideal op-amp can drive any load without losing voltage, so its output impedance is very low (ideally zero). Specific values like 100 or 1000 ohms are not characteristics of the ideal device.
A-002-008-007: Op-amp active filters provide gain instead of the insertion loss of passive LC filters, so the signal can come out stronger rather than weaker. Op-amps also avoid bulky inductors, but the key listed advantage over LC is gain rather than loss.
A-002-008-008: In amateur gear op-amp RC active filters are mainly used as audio filters in receivers, for example to sharpen CW or SSB passbands. They are not used for power-supply smoothing or as RF high/low-pass filters, since op-amps work at audio, not RF, frequencies.
A-002-008-009: An inverting op-amp circuit feeds the signal to the inverting input so the output is 180 degrees out of phase with the input. In-phase operation is the non-inverting configuration, so that is the tempting opposite answer.
A-002-008-010: A non-inverting op-amp circuit produces an output that is in phase with the input, feeding the signal to the non-inverting input. The 180-degrees-out-of-phase option describes the inverting configuration instead, so it is the distractor to avoid.
A-002-008-011: A high-gain, direct-coupled differential amplifier whose characteristics are set by external components is, by definition, an operational amplifier. Difference, summing, and plain high-gain audio amplifiers are narrower or different terms, so 'operational amplifier' is the best fit.
A-002-009-001: Mixing combines two signals in a non-linear device to produce new sum and difference frequencies, the heart of frequency conversion in receivers and transmitters. Recovering intelligence is detection, and the noise-elimination options describe other processes, so they are wrong.
A-002-009-002: A mixer's output contains the two original input frequencies plus their sum and difference frequencies. The 1.414/0.707 and square-root options are not how mixing works, so the original-plus-sum-and-difference answer is correct.
A-002-009-003: Too much signal drives the mixer beyond its linear range, generating unwanted spurious signals (intermodulation products) and 'birdies.' This overload does not produce helpful limiting or a clean beat note, so spurious signals is the right consequence.
A-002-009-004: A series capacitor passes the RF input signal while blocking the DC bias, so it acts as a DC blocking (coupling) capacitor at the base. It is not part of the tuned circuit here nor a bypass to ground, since it is in the signal path, not across it.
A-002-009-005: A frequency multiplier needs a harmonic-rich output, so it is run in Class C, whose short conduction angle and heavy distortion generate strong harmonics that a tuned circuit then selects. The more linear classes (A, AB, B) produce too few harmonics to multiply efficiently.
A-002-009-006: The variable capacitor C2 forms a resonant tank with L1, and adjusting it tunes that tank to the desired harmonic (multiple) of the input frequency so only that harmonic is selected. Tuning to the input frequency or providing feedback would defeat the multiplier's purpose.
A-002-009-007: C3 connects the supply end of L1 to ground for RF, providing an RF ground at the VCC connection point so the supply line looks like ground to the signal. It does not resonate with L1 or bypass audio; its job is to keep RF out of the power supply.
A-002-009-008: L1 with the variable capacitor C2 forms the resonant output tank that selects the chosen harmonic, making the stage act as a frequency multiplier. It is not a divider, voltage divider, or doubler in the DC sense; it is a tuned circuit picking a higher harmonic.
A-002-009-009: If a circuit's tuned components resonate at a higher frequency than the signal applied to it, it is selecting a harmonic, which is the signature of a frequency multiplier. A plain amplifier or linear stage stays on the same frequency, so the higher-frequency tuning points to multiplication.
A-002-009-010: C3 ties the supply end of L1 to ground for RF, so it functions as an RF bypass capacitor keeping signal energy out of the power supply. It is in shunt (to ground), not in the signal path, so it is not a DC-blocking, coupling, or tuning capacitor.
A-002-009-011: A mixer combines the input with a local oscillator to produce a sum or difference, allowing a shift from 5.3 MHz to 14.3 MHz (which is not an integer multiple). A frequency multiplier can only reach integer multiples, and 14.3 is not a whole-number multiple of 5.3, so a mixer is needed.
A-002-010-001: A NAND gate is an AND followed by an inverter, so its output goes low (logic 0) only when every input is high (logic 1); for all other input combinations the output is 1. The option giving 1 only when all inputs are 1 describes a plain AND gate, so that is the tempting wrong answer.
A-002-010-002: An OR gate outputs logic 1 if any input is logic 1; it is only 0 when all inputs are 0. The options that require all inputs the same describe AND or NOR behaviour, so 'any input high gives 1' is the OR rule.
A-002-010-003: A NOR gate is an OR followed by an inverter, so its output is 0 if any or all inputs are 1, and only goes to 1 when every input is 0. The option giving 0 only when all inputs are 0 is backwards, so watch the inversion.
A-002-010-004: A NOT gate (inverter) has one input and flips its logic level, so a logic 1 input gives a logic 0 output. The data-transmission descriptions are about gating, not inversion, so the simple level-flip definition is correct.
A-002-010-005: An EXCLUSIVE OR (XOR) gate outputs logic 1 only when its inputs differ, i.e. when one and only one input is 1. If both inputs are 1 or both are 0 the output is 0, so the 'all inputs 1' option describes an AND, not an XOR.
A-002-010-006: An EXCLUSIVE NOR (XNOR) gate is the inverse of XOR, so it outputs logic 1 when its inputs are the same, including when all inputs are 1. The option giving 1 for a single 1 input describes XOR behaviour, the opposite, so it is the distractor.
A-002-010-007: An AND gate outputs logic 1 only when all of its inputs are logic 1; any 0 input forces the output to 0. The options that give 1 from a single 1 or from all 0s describe OR or NOR behaviour, so they are wrong.
A-002-010-008: A flip-flop is a binary sequential logic element with two stable states, holding a single bit of memory until clocked or set. 'Binary' means two states, so the four-, eight-, or one-state options contradict the definition.
A-002-010-009: A bistable multivibrator has two stable states and is exactly what we call a flip-flop. Astable and monostable multivibrators give clocks and one-shots, but the bistable form is the flip-flop, so the gate options are wrong.
A-002-010-010: A latch is another name for a flip-flop, a one-bit memory element that holds (latches) its state. A decade counter is built from flip-flops but is not itself a latch, and an OR gate or op-amp store nothing, so flip-flop is correct.
A-002-010-011: In a multivibrator the two transistors alternate, so when one conducts (saturates) the cross-coupling drives the other into cut-off (off). 'Saturated' describes the conducting transistor, not the other one, so cut off is the correct state of the partner.
A-002-011-001: A crystal lattice filter uses several quartz crystals arranged so their combined high-Q response gives a narrow bandwidth with steep (sharp) skirts, ideal for selecting one SSB or CW signal. Wide-bandwidth or shallow-skirt descriptions miss the point of using crystals, which is sharpness.
A-002-011-002: The bandwidth and response shape of a crystal lattice filter are set by the relative frequencies of the individual crystals; spacing them apart widens the passband and changes the skirt shape. The centre frequency, following gain, and signal amplitude do not determine the filter's shape.
A-002-011-003: A good SSB voice filter passes about 2.4 kHz, just enough for intelligible speech while rejecting adjacent signals. 500 Hz is for CW, while 6 kHz or 15 kHz are far too wide for SSB and would let in interference, so 2.4 kHz is correct.
A-002-011-004: A crystal oscillator's quartz element has an extremely high Q and a very precise mechanical resonance, giving it much greater frequency stability than a tuned LC oscillator. Simplicity and harmonic behaviour are not its advantage; rock-solid frequency stability is.
A-002-011-005: A quartz crystal has an enormously high Q (thousands), far higher than any LC circuit, which is why it makes such a sharp, narrow bandpass filter. A low-Q crystal or a high-Q LC circuit would not give that selectivity, so the crystal's high Q is the reason.
A-002-011-006: The piezoelectric effect means a voltage appears when certain crystals are mechanically deformed (and they flex when a voltage is applied). Magnets and impurities have nothing to do with piezoelectricity, so 'deforming certain crystals' is the correct cause.
A-002-011-007: Electrically a quartz crystal behaves like a very high Q tuned (resonant) LC circuit, which is why it gives such sharp selectivity and stable oscillation. It is not a variable capacitance or a low-Q circuit; its hallmark is its extremely high Q.
A-002-011-008: An overtone oscillator deliberately operates the crystal near an integral multiple (overtone) of its fundamental frequency to reach higher frequencies directly. A crystal multiplier multiplies after oscillation, while lattice and ladder are filter types, so overtone oscillator is correct.
A-002-011-009: Crystals give excellent frequency stability, accuracy, and low noise because of their high Q, but they cannot deliver high power output, so high power output is the property that does not apply. The crystal is a frequency-determining element, not a power source.
A-002-011-010: Crystal oscillators, filters, and crystal microphones all rely on the piezoelectric effect, the link between mechanical deformation and voltage in certain crystals. Ferro-resonance and the made-up 'Hertzberg' and 'overtone' effects do not explain all three devices, so piezoelectric is correct.
A-002-011-011: Crystals are used in oscillators, lattice filters, and microphones, but not in active filters, which are built from op-amps and RC networks instead. So 'active filters' is the application where crystals do not apply.
A-002-012-001: The three general filter groupings by what band they pass are high-pass, low-pass, and band-pass. Hartley/Colpitts/Pierce are oscillator types, and the other lists name circuit elements, not filter categories, so high/low/band-pass is correct.
A-002-012-002: A Butterworth filter is designed for a maximally flat response across its passband, with no ripple, at the cost of a more gradual cutoff. The constant-impedance-product description fits a constant-k filter, and the conductor/capacitor-only claims are wrong, so maximally flat is the defining feature.
A-002-012-003: A Chebyshev filter trades allowable ripple in the passband for a sharper, steeper cutoff than a Butterworth. The ripple-with-sharp-cutoff description fits Chebyshev specifically, while Butterworth is flat with a gentler roll-off, so it is the distractor.
A-002-012-004: A Chebyshev filter deliberately allows ripple in the passband in exchange for steeper skirts (faster cutoff) than a Butterworth. The maximally-flat description belongs to the Butterworth, and the inductor/capacitor-only claims are wrong, so the ripple-for-steepness trade is its hallmark.
A-002-012-005: Resonant cavities act as very high-Q, narrow bandpass filters at VHF and higher frequencies, where their physical size is practical. They are not used as power-line, low-pass, or high-pass filters, so the narrow VHF bandpass role is correct.
A-002-012-006: A quarter-wavelength cavity is one quarter of the free-space wavelength: λ = 300 / f(MHz) = 300 / 50 = 6 metres, so λ/4 = 6 / 4 = 1.5 metres (about 5 ft). The other lengths come from using the wrong fraction of a wavelength, so divide the wavelength by four.
A-002-012-007: A helical resonator is a compact high-Q tuned circuit installed in a receiver front end to cut overload and spurious responses at VHF, UHF, and above. A diplexer, directional coupler, or duplexer route or sample signals rather than filtering the front end, so helical resonator is correct.
A-002-012-008: A television channel is about 6 MHz wide, which is far too broad for a high-Q resonant cavity (those give very narrow passbands) yet awkward for Butterworth or Chebyshev LC filters at VHF and above. Since no single listed filter is a good fit for that wide a bandwidth at these frequencies, the correct answer is 'none of the other answers.'
A-002-012-009: The Butterworth filter's primary advantage is its maximally flat response across the passband, with no ripple. The ripple-for-steeper-skirts trait belongs to the Chebyshev, so it is the opposite and a tempting distractor.
A-002-012-010: The Chebyshev filter's primary advantage over Butterworth is steeper skirts (faster cutoff), achieved by allowing some ripple in the passband. The maximally-flat response is the Butterworth's trait, so it is the opposite and the distractor to avoid.
A-002-012-011: A cavity filter relies on physical resonance whose size is only practical at VHF and above, so it is not suitable at audio and low radio frequencies. Elliptical, Chebyshev, and Butterworth filters can all be built with lumped components for low frequencies, so the cavity is the odd one out.
